title: Brain Aneurysm | MedlinePlus
content: Brain Aneurysm Also called: Berry aneurysm, Cerebral aneurysm, Intracranial aneurysm On this page Basics Summary Start Here Symptoms Diagnosis and Tests Treatments and Therapies Learn More Living With Related Issues Specifics See, Play and Learn Images Videos and Tutorials Research Statistics and Research Clinical Trials Journal Articles Resources Find an Expert For You Patient Handouts Summary A brain aneurysm is an abnormal bulge or "ballooning" in the wall of an artery in the brain. They are sometimes called berry aneurysms because they are often the size of a small berry. Most brain aneurysms produce no symptoms until they become large, begin to leak blood, or burst. If a brain aneurysm presses on nerves in your brain, it can cause signs and symptoms. These can include: A droopy eyelid Double vision or other changes in vision Pain above or behind the eye A dilated pupil Numbness or weakness on one side of the face or body Treatment depends on the size and location of the aneurysm, whether it is infected, and whether it has burst. If a brain aneurysm bursts, symptoms can include a sudden, severe headache, nausea and vomiting, stiff neck, loss of consciousness, and signs of a stroke . Any of these symptoms requires immediate medical attention.
